unit.name: Evaluation goals differ across the ML lifecycle
unit.description: Introduces why evaluation is needed at training, development, model selection, deployment, and publishing, and why each stage wants different metric properties.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by framing evaluation as a stage-dependent design problem rather than a single universal metric. During training, metrics must be fast, cheap, differentiable, and resistant to shortcut optimization [ts=63s] [ts=176s]. During deployment, the requirements shift toward trustworthiness, task specificity, and absolute thresholds because production decisions need go/no-go judgments rather than just pairwise comparisons [ts=240s] [ts=266s]. The publishing setting is treated separately: academic benchmarks should be reproducible, standardized, easy to run, and directionally useful over years even if the metric is imperfect, with benchmark design balancing simplicity against difficulty so the task is neither random nor already saturated [ts=283s] [ts=332s] [ts=373s].

key_points.text: Training, development, model selection, deployment, and publishing each require evaluation, but not with the same metric properties.
Training-time metrics should be fast, cheap, differentiable, and hard to game through shortcuts.
Deployment metrics should be trustworthy, task-specific, and absolute enough to support thresholds like 'do not ship below 95%'.
Academic benchmarks should be reproducible and standardized so papers remain comparable over many years.
A useful academic benchmark balances difficulty and simplicity: too hard gives random performance, too easy gives saturated baselines.

kp_catalog.name: Evaluation objectives by lifecycle stage
kp_catalog.description: Different phases such as training, development, deployment, and publishing need different evaluation properties.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because evaluation objectives by lifecycle stage formalizes how different phases such as training, development, deployment, and publishing need different evaluation properties, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Academic benchmark design tradeoffs
kp_catalog.description: Academic benchmarks prioritize reproducibility, standardization, ease of use, and directional progress over perfect realism.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because academic benchmark design tradeoffs clarifies academic benchmarks prioritize reproducibility, standardization, ease of use, and directional progress over perfect realism, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
